title: Coleus klossii
intro: Coleus klossii, also known as Coleus, is a species of flowering plant in the family Lamiaceae, native to tropical regions of Africa and Asia. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal issues, due to its potential anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Coleus klossii include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made with Coleus klossii include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat fever, colds, and other minor health complaints.
taxonomy: Coleus klossii is a species of flowering plant in the family Lamiaceae, commonly known as the mint or deadnettle family. It is native to tropical Africa and is often grown as an ornamental plant for its vibrant foliage. This species is a member of the genus Coleus, which consists of over 300 species of flowering plants. Coleus klossii is often classified as a synonym of Solenostemon klossii, due to the varying classification systems used by botanists. The plant's classification can be further refined using the APG III system, which groups it within the clade Asterids.
